pages.text: The effect of mineral admixtures on mechanical properties, chloride ion permeability and impermeability of self-compacting concrete Mucteba Uysal a,⇑, Kemalettin Yilmaz a, Metin Ipek b a Sakarya University, Civil Engineering Department, 54187 Sakarya, Turkey b Sakarya University, Construction Education Department, 54187 Sakarya, Turkey article info Article history: Received 12 November 2010 Received in revised form 21 July 2011 Accepted 23 July 2011 Available online 20 August 2011 Keywords: SCC Mechanical properties Impermeability Chloride ion permeability abstract The objective of this study was to evaluate the effectiveness of various mineral admixtures in producing self-compacting concrete (SCC). For this purpose, ﬂy ash (FA), granulated blast furnace slag (GBFS), lime- stone powder (LP), basalt powder (BP) and marble powder (MP) were used. Workability of SCC was deter- mined using slump ﬂow, T 50 time, L-box and V-funnel tests. The hardened properties that were determined included ultrasonic pulse velocity and compressive strength determined at 7 and 28 days. It was concluded that among the mineral admixtures used, FA and GBSF signiﬁcantly increased the workability of SCC. Dura- bility properties of SCC mixtures such as, chloride ion permeability and water impermeability have been investigated besides mechanical properties within the scope this study. Test results indicated that SCC could be obtained with not only LP which the most common ﬁller material in SCC applications but also BP and MP as ﬁller materials. Replacing 20% of Portland cement (PC) with GBFS resulted in strength of more than 78 MPa at 28 days. Furthermore, the best resistance to chloride ion permeability was obtained from a combination of 60% GBFS with 40% PC. On the other hand, impermeability depth test results were in the range of 4.42–12.58 mm. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Self-compacting concrete (SCC) is a special concrete type that can settle into the heavily reinforced, deep and narrow sections by its own weight, and can consolidate itself without necessitating inter- nal or external vibration, and while providing with these features can keep its cohesion (stability) without leading segregation and bleeding [1]. This concrete type developed in 1980 in Japan with the progressions in the concrete technologies has become wide- spread in all over the world. Especially the developments in the superplasticizer technology have contributed signiﬁcantly to forma- tion and progression of the SCC [2–4]. Like conventional vibrated concrete, SCC contains small quantities of superplasticizer. Further- more, in order to avoid separation of large particles in SCC, pozzola- nic admixtures or ﬁllers to increase the viscosity are used [5]. SCC demands a large amount of powder content compared to conven- tional vibrated concrete to produce a homogeneous and cohesive mix [6]. It was reported that SCC often contains powder in the order of 450–600 kg/m 3 of concrete [7]. Due to its rheological require- ments, ﬁllers (both reactive and inert) are commonly used in SCC to improve and maintain the workability, as well as to regulate the cement content and to reduce the heat of hydration. Part of this pow- der content can be effectively replaced by mineral admixtures like FA, GBFS, LP, etc. [8–12]. Without dependence on the fact that SCC consists basically of the same components as normal vibrated con- crete, there exist distinct differences regarding the

It is also known that FA and GBFS increase the workability, durability and long-term properties of SCC [17,18]. The use of FA or GBFS could increase the slump of the concrete mixture without increasing its cost. The use of FA or GBFS also reduces the demand for cement, sand and ﬁne ﬁllers which are required in SCC. Moreover, incorporating FA or GBFS also eliminates the need for viscosity-enhanced chemical admixtures. The lower water content of the concrete results in high- er durability, in addition to a better mechanical integrity of the structure [19]. The use of FA and GBFS as partial replacement of or- dinary PC improves strength and durability of SCC by creating a den- ser matrix and thereby increasing durability of concrete structures [20]. LP has been used in concrete production for the last 25 years[21], not only to increase the service life of concrete, but also for the main purposes of lowering the costs and environmental load of cement production. More recently LP is also used as a ﬁller material to im- prove the workability and stability of fresh concrete. For a high ﬂow- able concrete, such as SCC, LP is added to increase the volume of the continuous phase of lubricating paste and the packing of the granu- lar skeleton, bind (physically) excess water [22]. SCC has to possess two incompatible properties: high segregation resistance and high workability. This balance is made possible by the dispersing effect of high-range water-reducing admixture (superplasticizer) com- bined with cohesiveness of high concentration of ﬁne particles in additional ﬁller material [23]. The main mechanisms controlling this ﬁne balance are related to surface physics and chemistry; hence SCC is heavily dependent on surface activity of the admixtures together with the high speciﬁc surface area generated by the ﬁne materials [24]. Nowadays, disposal of the waste materials of the marble indus- try, consisting of very ﬁne powders, is one of the environmental problems all over the world [1]. MP is a waste material with lime- stone origin and Turkey has foremost marble reserves in the world. As waste in Turkey, 511.000 tons of MP per year are left and stored on depot areas [25]. Stocking of MP is not feasible. These type solid waste materials should be inactivated in a proper manner without polluting the environment. The most suitable inactivating method nowadays is recycling. Recycling provides with some beneﬁts such as energy saving, protecting the natural resources and contributing to economy, decreasing the waste materials and investing for the future [1,26]. On the other hand, it is well known that LP as ﬁller material in SCC shows quite good performance [27,28]. Thus, it would be proﬁtable if MP can be used in SCC as a ﬁller material and thereby as a by-product for the concrete industry. Topcu et al. have investigated fresh and hardened properties of MP addi- tive SCC and the use of MP amount below 200 kg/m 3 content is suitable for improving fresh and hardened properties of SCC [25]. In the experimental program of the study, a series of SCC mixes were produced. For this purpose, mineral admixtures such as, FA, GBFS, LP, BP and MP have been replaced with cement various rates. A constant water/binder (w/b) ratio (0.33) has been used at all mixtures to compare the mixtures. Then, several tests such as slump-ﬂow, T 50 time, L-box and V-funnel tests were applied for fresh concrete and some tests such as compressive strength, den- sity and ultrasonic pu

Water impermeability of SCC mixtures was determined by using water imper- meability test equipment ( Fig. 1 ) according to DIN 1048 standard [31]. SCC speci- mens were placed impermeability test equipment and tubes ﬁlled with water. Scale readings of tubes were measured by applying water ﬁrstly and then the sam- ples were put into under water pressure 48 h 1 bar, 24 h 3 bars and 24 h 7 bars. Sec- ondly, scale readings have been completed and the amount of water absorbed and the weight of the samples were determined. By the end of the test, splitting test was applied on samples depth of water impermeability found. 3. Results and discussion The results of fresh concrete tests, compressive strength and ultrasonic pulse velocity of test specimens at 7 and 28 days, den- sity, static and dynamic elastic moduli at 28 days, chloride ion per- meability and water impermeability at 28 days with different series of SCC are discussed in the following paragraphs. 3.1. Properties of fresh concrete The results of fresh properties of all SCC mixtures are discussed in this part. Some experiments such as slump-ﬂow, V-funnel, L-box and T 50 tests are conducted for discussing the workability of SCC. Comparing the results with the SCC criteria, it can be seen that all designed mixtures exhibited satisfactory properties as fresh concrete. The conventional slump test was not appropriate to dis- cuss the workability of SCC as a high performance concrete. The slump-ﬂow test is a value-system for the ability of concrete to de- form under its own weight against the friction of the surface with no external restraint present [32]. It can be seen from the Fig. 2 , slump ﬂow diameters vary between 690 mm and 750 mm. FA35 showed highest slump ﬂowability (750 mm), when com- paring to other mixtures. Since, the water demand and workability are controlled by particle shape, particle packing effect, particle size distribution and the smoothness of surface texture. The electron images of mineral admixtures presented in Fig. 3, as seen from the images, when compared to the other mineral admixtures, the FA particles had a spherical geometry. The spherical shapes re- duce the friction at the aggregate-paste interface producing a ‘‘ball- bearing effect’’ at the point of contact [33]. So, the mixtures with only FA replacement performed better probably because of the common explanation of FA having a special ‘‘shape effect’’ due to its spherical particles which easily roll over one another, reducing inter-particle friction and therefore increasing workability. One an- other possible explanation is such that, a partial replacement of ce- ment by FA results in higher volume of paste due to its lower density and this increase in the paste volume reduces the friction at the ﬁne aggregate-paste interface and improves the plasticity and cohesiveness, and thus leads to increased workability [27]. Other mixtures which containing ﬁller materials had the reduction in workability when comparing to FA. The nodular and angular shape of LP, BP and MP which hinder the workability of the SCC. This explains the relatively negative effect of LP, BP and MP on the fresh concrete properties. The relationship between ﬂowing through V-funnel and the re- quired times for reaching 50 cm slump-ﬂow (T 50) to determine the Table 2 Mix proportions of SCC for 1 m 3. Materials (kg/m 3) Cement FA GBFS LP BP MP Water w/c w/p Sand CSI CSII Control 550 – – – – – 182 0.33 0.33 869 467 311 UK15 467 83 – – – – 182 0.

the appropriate SCC mixture. However, in V-funnel test all SCCs showed ﬂow time values in the range of 9.34–16.97 s. All of the SCC mixtures showed the required times for reaching 50 cm slump-ﬂow (T 50) values in the range of 2–5 s which means EFNARC Committee suggests that 2 and 5 s for lower and upper limits of T 50 time. The L-box ratio characterizes the ﬁlling and passing ability of SCC. There is generally a blocking risk of the mixture when the L-box blocking ratio is below 0.8 [29]. The blocking ratio of SCC produced with various mineral admixtures is given in Fig. 5 . The blocking ratio (h 2/h1) should be between 0.8 and 1.00. All mixtures of SCC are within this target range. When mineral admixtures con- tent have increased, it has not negatively affected the blocking ra- tio because of the concurrent decrease in viscosity [25]. However, it can be noted that each SCC investigated in the present study has adequate ﬁlling capability and passing ability. 3.2. Hardened properties The hardened properties that were determined for all 16 mix- tures were the density at 28 days, compressive strength and UPV at 7 and 28 days. Table 3 presents the density, compressive strength and UPV as determined from three specimens. Generally speaking, as the amount of mineral admixtures increased the compressive strength further decreased for all the series. At 7 days, the test re- sults showed that the compressive strength for the control mixture was higher than the mixtures containing mineral admixtures indi- cating that the pozzolanic reactions of FA and GBFS were not sufﬁ- cient to enhance compressive strength at this early stage. But, the slower pozzolanic reactions came into play and the GBSF series had the highest compressive strength results at 28 days. In the case Fig. 4. Relationship between V-funnel time and T 50 time for SCC mixtures. Fig. 5. Blocking ratio of SCC versus mineral admixture type at different replacement rates. Compressive strength of SCC versus mineral admixture type at different replacement rates (a) at 7 days and (b) at 28 days. M. Uysal et al. / Construction and Building Materials 27 (2012) 263–270 267

of GBFS and FA, ﬁlling of the voids between the larger cement parti- cles, and increasing production of secondary hydrates by pozzolanic reactions with the lime resulting from the primary hydration en- hances compressive strength. Here the role of LP and BP are also bet- ter understood as it only acts like inert ﬁller reducing the compressive strength of the LP and BP series. But, MP10 mixture showed the best performance at 7 days as early age. This is about the physical nature of better packing, by addition of MP governs the compressive strength due to the denser matrix and the better dispersion of cement grains [28]. Furthermore, the surfaces of the MP will act as nucleation sites for the early reaction products of CH and CSH, which will accelerate the hydration of cement clinkers (especially C 3S) and consequently increase the early age compres- sive strength [34,35]. MP is the most ﬁneness material in all the mix- tures. The effect of nucleation on the strength is dependent on the ﬁller’s afﬁnity to cement hydrates, and it increases with ﬁneness and speciﬁc surface area of the ﬁller. MP is not pozzolan, but nor fully inert as it reacts with the alumina phases of the cement. If the ce- ment has a signiﬁcant amount of tricalcium aluminate (C 3A), cal- cium carboaluminate will be produced from the reaction between calcium carbonate (CaCO 3) from the MP and the C 3A [36–38]. This reaction, accelerating the hydration and increasing the compressive strength, increases with the C3A content of the cement and the ﬁne- ness and speciﬁc surface area of the ﬁller. However, MP caused an increasing of early age performance of SCC. Fig. 6 presents compres- sive strength of SCC mixtures. Fig. 7 presents the relationship between the compressive strength and the ultrasonic pulse velocity of SCC mixtures. The trend in V is similar to that of compressive strength. Ultrasonic velocity has increased by increasing compressive strength for all the mixtures. The reduction in the UPV was just about in line with the reduction in the compressive strength. The correlation be- tween the compressive strength and UPV is quite strong ( R = 0.85 for all the mixtures). Fig. 8 presents static and dynamic elastic moduli according to mineral admixture type at different replacement rates. Static elas- tic modulus was measured according to ASTM C 469 [39]. The fol- lowing equation is used to represent dynamic elastic modulus of concrete. Ed ¼ 105 /C2 V2 /C2ð D=gÞð 1Þ where Ed is the dynamic elastic modulus (GPa), V is ultrasonic pulse velocity (m/s), D is unit weight of specimen (kg/dm 3) and g is gravity (9.81 m/s 2). If the strength of concrete increases, static and dynamic elastic moduli increase [40]. The test results indicated that this situation was similar in SCC and the highest static and dynamic elastic moduli had been obtained for GBFS20 mixture. Static and dy- namic elastic moduli of the SCC decreased signiﬁcantly when increasing the replacement level of mineral admixtures. 3.3. Durability properties Rapid chloride ion permeability test was conducted on all the SCC. The total charge passing in 6 h as a measure of the chloride permeability is presented in Fig. 9 . The chloride ion permeability limits suggested by ASTM C1202 were compared with the results. The chloride ion permeability of the SCC containing mineral admixtures decreased when replacement rate of mineral admix- tures regardless of mineral admixture type was increased. The re- sults showed that GBFS6

pozzolanic admixtures such as, FA and GBFS showed lower chlo- ride ion permeability results comparing to ﬁllers. Similar results were observed by other researchers [41–44]. The formation of a less porous, denser microstructure and a discontinuous pore system becomes critical for reduced chloride ion permeability. The pozzolanic reactions seem to be able to develop a discontinu- ous pore system more readily. This could be the due to the fact that the chloride ion penetration depends on the chloride binding capacity of the constituent materials. Usually chlorides penetrate in concrete by diffusion along water paths or open pores. Some of these chlorides can react with the cement compounds, mainly tricalciumaluminates (C 3A), forming stable chlorocomplexes. The excess of chloride is free and leads to the initiation of the corrosion process [19]. The presence of FA and GBFS lead to an increase in the amount of C 3A due to the higher amount of alumina present in the mix and to an increase in the content of calcium silicate hydrate that is formed in the pozzolanic reactions. Thus, the chloride bind- ing capacity of concrete tends to increase with FA and GBFS addi- tion and consequently less free chloride is available to initiate the corrosion process, as observed by some researchers [45]. It can be clearly observed that the alumina content has a signiﬁcant inﬂu- ence on the total charge of the concrete. As the alumina (AI 2O3) content increases the total charge decreases. The mixtures incorpo- rating more FA and GBFS content (with high amounts of alumina contents) exhibited a much better resistance to chloride ion pene- tration. It was reported signiﬁcant reduction of chloride ion perme- ability of SCC with pozzolanic admixtures [46]. Fig. 10 presents water impermeability depth of SCC mixtures. Water impermeability depth test results were in the range of 4.42–12.58 mm. The results indicated that GBFS20 mixture showed the lowest water impermeability depth. Moreover, as the amount of mineral admixtures increased impermeability depth also increased. Control, FA15 and GBFS20 mixtures had lower impermeability depth when comparing to other mixtures. However, cement content had more pronounced than mineral admixture content on the water impermeability depth of SCC. This test was performed at 28 days and pozzolanic reactions did not start exactly and the productions of sec- ondary hydrates were not sufﬁcient to exhibit a much better resis- tance to water impermeability by ﬁlling the pores. Nevertheless, FA15 and GBFS20 mixtures did not allow passing water by ﬁlling the pores. 4. Conclusions This study was conducted to assess the effect of mineral admix- tures on the properties SCC. The following conclusions can be drawn from the present study: /C15 Among the mineral admixtures considered, the best perfor- mance has been obtained for FA series as workability proper- ties. In general, the use of mineral admixtures improved signiﬁcantly the workability properties of SCC. To evaluate workability properties of SCC mixtures as slump-ﬂow, T 50 time, V-funnel time and L-box ratio tests used, it can be seen that all SCC mixtures have remained in target range for each test except V-funnel time. /C15 The point of the highest compressive strength has been obtained for MP10 mixture at the early age. After 7 days, this point moves toward GBFS series. The addition of LP and BP caused a reduction in both early and ultimate ages of compressive strength. UPV could be

segregation resistance of fresh SCC produced with mineral admix- tures, is shown in Fig. 4. V-funnel test can evaluate the viscosity in relative terms only under the condition of the constant slump ﬂow value. In such a case, a longer ﬂow time represents a higher viscos- ity of the mixture and it directly relates to a better resistance to segregation. Both the required times for reaching 50 cm slump- ﬂow (T 50) and the V-funnel ﬂow times are in good agreement to that of the values given by European guidelines for range of appli- cations and for different viscosity classes. On the other hand, most of the series have good viscosities and segregation resistances by taking part in target range. GBFS60 mixture has the lowest viscosities and V-funnel ﬂow times compared to other mixtures. EFNARC Committee suggests that 11 and 15 s for lower and upper limits of V-funnel time, respectively, are acceptable for designing Fig. 3. Secondary electron images of the mineral admixtures: (a) smooth FA particles (3000 /C2 ), (b) and (c) rough and angular GBFS and LP particles (3000 /C2 ), (d) and (e) rough and angular BP and MP particles (3500 /C2 ). 266 M.
